text: number of reasons beyond our present discussion. The book is composed of a series of small stories and must be read as van Gennep’s sharp, humoristic and ironic denouement of French academic life. It has barely received attention within France. The general interest in French ethnology was very much due to the high status that Claude Lévi-Strauss and his structuralist approach had attained. Lévi-Strauss had himself recognized the value of van Gennep’s early work in his book on totemism, remarking upon van Gennep’s ‘unusual theoretical boldness’ (Lévi- Strauss 1963: 4). Anglo-Saxon readers must have noticed with some curiosity how Lévi-Strauss sets out his own discussion with reference to Arnold van Gennep – and not to Durkheim. As we saw, it was indeed van Gennep who had translated Frazer’s book on totemism into French. Lévi-Strauss simply had to recognize his expertise and the potential of his analytical approach, clearly seeing van Gennep as a better starting point than Durkheim. Edmund Leach thought likewise. In a 1968 overview essay on ritual, Leach bluntly stated that ‘van Gennep’s schema has proved more useful than Durkheim’s’ (1968: 522). This was new. Liminality and the Modern 72 Leach’s recognition of van Gennep’s superiority to Durkheim is far from strange, as Leach had himself paid great attention to the ‘rhythmical’, seasonal and ritualistic changes of roles and even kinship terms and names in his own ethnographic work (Leach 1954). Lévi-Strauss’ (albeit partial) recognition of van Gennep is also not strange, for van Gennep always insisted that ceremonial patterns should be examined as wholes and that comparison should be based upon similarities in structure rather than upon content. Because van Gennep never became established in French academic life, he also by and large failed to have an impact outside France. He did become an important figure in Swiss, and of course French, folklore. However, the discipline of folklore was never something with a large reach. In France it was not even a university discipline until 1943. From the early 1920s, van Gennep was mostly working on his own, building up a discipline which had no official scientific status, no curriculum, and therefore no students. That was his destiny, but he coped with it and kept on working as long as he lived. This almost total lack of recognition meant that in post-Durkheimian anthropology, the concept of liminality was entirely absent. Besides Henri Junod, one of the only anthropologists of importance to discuss Rites of Passage before its translation in 1960 was Paul Radin in his Primitive Religion (1937). Radin positively referred to van Gennep’s work on totemism (pp. 203–4), and Chapter 5 of Primitive Religion, on The Crisis of Life and Transition Rites, was quite simply an application of van Gennep’s framework. 1 Radin also anticipated some of Turner’s ideas: Chapter 14 was on ‘ritual drama’ (the title of the chapter). This is a significant exception, for as we shall argue below, Radin’s work on the trickster greatly complements the analytical vigour of liminality as tied to crisis situations. However, this application was more like a single rupture in a sea of amnesia. The general fact is that van Gennep became known to Anglo-Saxon anthropology only with the 1960 translation of his book. He was not on anyone’s reading list before that; and in that very concrete sense, as happened with Tarde, Durkheim’s triumph had been total. The English Translation of Rites of Passage The English version of Rites of Passage today exists in five different editions, those of 1960, 1977, 1980, 2004 and, most recently, 2010 (a reprint of the 1977 edition). The 1960 edition by Routledge and Kegan Paul, printed by the Chicago University Press in America, was the decisive one. It quite suddenly made 1 In Turner’s own brief overview of van Gennep’s reception (1985: 158–9), he mentions the work of E. D. Chappell and C. S. Cohn, who in their 1942 Principles of Anthropology discussed rites of passage; drawing on Kimball, Turner also makes mention of scholarship in psychology which had applied van Gennep’s scheme toward the analysis of individual development. For the linkage between liminality and contemporary affectivity literature, see Thomassen (2013c). Liminality Rediscovered: With Victor Turner and Beyond 73 Arnold van Gennep known in Anglo-Saxon academia. The reviews were almost overwhelming. His original book from 1909 had in fact been reviewed quite positively in both English and American journals, but everyone had forgotten about that. If anyone remembered anything, then it was Mauss’ dismissive review. The book had drowned in oblivion, and van Gennep’s name was simply not known. Only a very few anthropologists in the Anglo-Saxon world had made reference to the book – indeed very few anthropologists and sociologists as such had made reference to the book. Now everything changed. Unfortunately Arnold van Gennep did not live to see this belated reception of his book: he had died three years earlier, on 8 May 1957, at the age of 84. Rites of Passage is one of the books that since then has been quoted in the greatest variety of fields and disciplines; it is a fame that van Gennep knew nothing of while he was alive, living like a ‘hermit’. Outside academia the very concept, rite of passage, has become a household term in the entire English-speaking world – and this is a rare feat for an indeed highly academic book. As a book, Rites of Passage is in fact slightly difficult to approach for non-specialists. It is meticulously empirical and makes reference to a vast literature written in more than a dozen languages, and more than a century ago by now. Some of Van Gennep’s comparisons are made between tribal groups or peoples that were already near extinction at the moment of publication. Yet without a doubt this is one of the most successful books ever published in anthropology. And still the publication – and the larger reception – has its serious pitfalls. The initiative for the 1960 translation and publication was taken by Solon T. Kimball. The
